does: This paper introduces OrchJail, a fuzzing framework designed to jailbreak tool-calling text-to-image agents by exploiting vulnerabilities in how multiple tools are orchestrated together. Rather than attempting to bypass safety measures through prompt manipulation alone, OrchJail learns from successful jailbreak traces to identify high-risk tool-orchestration patterns—sequences where individually benign steps combine to produce harmful outputs. The framework uses this knowledge to guide fuzzing toward prompts more likely to trigger unsafe multi-step tool behaviors. Experiments demonstrate that OrchJail achieves higher attack success rates, better image fidelity, and lower query costs compared to existing jailbreak methods across representative tool-calling T2I agents while remaining robust against common defenses.
connects: This work builds on emerging research into safety vulnerabilities in multi-step AI systems and agent-based architectures. It extends prior work on prompt-based jailbreaking techniques by recognizing that tool-calling agents introduce a fundamentally new attack surface beyond traditional text-based safety measures. The paper connects to research on fuzzing methodologies adapted for language and vision systems, as well as studies examining how compositions of individually safe components can produce unsafe emergent behaviors. It also relates to work on understanding causal relationships between inputs and system behavior, applying this to systematically discover problematic tool-orchestration patterns that simpler prompt-only attacks would miss.
enables: This research opens several directions for both attack and defense. It highlights the need for safety mechanisms specifically designed for multi-step tool orchestration rather than just individual tool inputs. The work enables future research into detection methods that identify dangerous tool-calling sequences before execution, robustness testing frameworks for agent systems, and safer design patterns for tool-calling architectures. More broadly, it points toward understanding compositional safety properties of complex AI systems, where interactions between components create emergent risks. The methodology could be adapted to test and improve safety in other domains involving orchestrated tool use, such as code execution systems, robotics, and autonomous planning agents.
